2025 · arXiv · Foundation Models

Qwen3 Technical Report

Alibaba's Qwen3 family fuses "thinking" and "non-thinking" modes into single dense (0.6B–32B) and Mixture-of-Experts (30B-A3B, 235B-A22B) checkpoints, with a user-controllable thinking-budget dial and a 36-trillion-token, 119-language pre-training run. A distillation recipe pushes the lightweight sizes to strong-to-weak parity at roughly 1/10th the GPU hours of direct reinforcement learning.

Qwen Team — An Yang, Anfeng Li, Baosong Yang, Beichen Zhang, Binyuan Hui, et al. (60 authors)
venue arXiv citations 6,619 topic Foundation Models source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07-19
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By mid-2025 the open-weight ecosystem had split into two separate species of model: fast general-purpose chat models (GPT-4o, Qwen2.5) and dedicated reasoning models that spend far more inference-time compute on explicit chain-of-thought before answering (OpenAI's o3, DeepSeek-R1, and Alibaba's own QwQ-32B). Using both meant living with the seam between them — the report's own example is "switching from Qwen2.5 to QwQ" depending on whether the next query is a quick chat turn or a hard math problem. Every reasoning-vs-chat trade-off was a model swap, not a setting.

Qwen2.5 also had two structural gaps the report calls out directly: multilingual coverage stopped at 29 languages and dialects, and each of the smaller dense checkpoints needed its own full post-training run rather than inheriting capability cheaply from the flagship.

Qwen3's answer is to collapse the reasoning/chat split into one checkpoint with a runtime toggle — thinking mode and non-thinking mode fused into a single model, switchable per turn via a /think / /no_think flag, with a thinking budget that lets a user cap how many tokens the model spends reasoning before it has to commit to an answer — trading latency for quality on a sliding scale instead of a binary model choice.

// how it works

The method

The architecture is a conservative extension of Qwen2.5: Grouped-Query Attention, SwiGLU, Rotary Position Embeddings, and pre-norm RMSNorm all carry over; QKV-bias is dropped and QK-Norm is added to the attention mechanism "to ensure stable training." The lineup spans six dense checkpoints (0.6B, 1.7B, 4B, 8B, 14B, 32B) and two MoE checkpoints — Qwen3-30B-A3B (128 experts, 8 active) and the flagship Qwen3-235B-A22B (235B total / 22B activated) — sharing a 151,669-token byte-level BPE vocabulary. Unlike Qwen2.5-MoE, the Qwen3 MoE design drops shared experts and adds a global-batch load-balancing loss to encourage specialization.

Pre-training: 36T tokens, three stages

Pre-training data roughly doubled in volume and tripled in language coverage versus Qwen2.5 — 36 trillion tokens across 119 languages and dialects. Part of the corpus is synthesized rather than scraped: Qwen2.5-VL is fine-tuned to OCR large volumes of PDF documents, and Qwen2.5, Qwen2.5-Math, and Qwen2.5-Coder generate additional trillions of tokens as textbooks, QA pairs, and code. Training runs in three stages: a 30-trillion-token General Stage at a 4,096-token sequence length building broad world knowledge; a 5-trillion-token Reasoning Stage that up-weights STEM, coding, and synthetic reasoning data with an accelerated learning-rate decay; and a Long-Context Stage (hundreds of billions of tokens at 32,768-token sequence length, 75% of it between 16K–32K tokens) that combines RoPE base-frequency scaling (10,000 → 1,000,000, the ABF technique) with YARN and Dual Chunk Attention for a further 4× inference-time context extension.

Post-training: a four-stage pipeline, plus distillation for the small sizes

The flagship models go through four post-training stages (Figure 1). (1) Long-CoT Cold Start — SFT on math/code/logic/STEM problems with verified answers, where Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct filters out queries answerable without reasoning and QwQ-32B generates candidate solutions that are then filtered for repetition, guesswork, and thinking/summary inconsistency. (2) Reasoning RL — GRPO over 3,995 query-verifier pairs chosen to be unseen, learnable, maximally challenging, and broad-domain; this stage alone lifts the 235B model's AIME'24 score from 70.1 to 85.1 over 170 RL steps, with no manual hyperparameter intervention. (3) Thinking Mode Fusion — continual SFT that merges thinking and non-thinking data (thinking data is rejection-sampled from the Stage-2 model itself) and introduces the /think / /no_think chat-template flags. The thinking-budget mechanism is a side-effect of this stage, not something explicitly trained: once a user-set token limit is hit, a stop-thinking instruction is inserted and the model finishes from its partial reasoning — a capability the report says "emerges naturally." (4) General RL — a reward system spanning 20+ tasks (instruction following, format following, agent tool-calling, RAG grounding, preference alignment), scored by three reward types: rule-based (for objectively-checkable formats), model-based-with-reference (Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct scoring against a gold answer), and a trained preference reward model.

The six lightweight checkpoints — five dense sizes (0.6B–14B) plus the 30B-A3B MoE — skip this four-stage process and instead get Strong-to-Weak Distillation: an off-policy phase distilling teacher outputs (from both modes) into the student, followed by an on-policy phase that fine-tunes the student's own generated sequences by matching its logits to a teacher's (Qwen3-32B or 235B-A22B) to minimize KL divergence. The report's claim is that this "requires only 1/10 of the GPU hours compared to the four-stage training method" — a claim the results section backs with a direct head-to-head (see below).

// what they found

The results

The flagship, Qwen3-235B-A22B in thinking mode, posts 85.7 on AIME'24, 81.5 on AIME'25, 70.7 on LiveCodeBench v5, a 2,056 CodeForces rating (98.2nd percentile), and 70.8 on BFCL v3 — sitting between DeepSeek-R1 (79.8 AIME'24) and Gemini 2.5 Pro (92.0 AIME'24) among reasoning-focused frontier models, while activating only 22B of its 235B total parameters. On the base-model side, Qwen3-235B-A22B-Base beats DeepSeek-V3-Base on 14 of 15 pre-training benchmarks with about a third of the total parameters and two-thirds of the activated ones; Qwen3-32B-Base — with under half the parameters — beats Qwen2.5-72B-Base on 10 of 15.

Qwen3-235B-A22B (thinking)Score
AIME'2485.7
AIME'2581.5
LiveCodeBench v570.7
CodeForces (rating / pct.)2056 / 98.2%
BFCL v370.8

The report's sharpest efficiency claim is the on-policy-distillation-vs-RL ablation on Qwen3-8B (Table 21): starting from the same off-policy-distilled checkpoint, continuing with on-policy distillation reaches 74.4 AIME'24 pass@1 (93.3 pass@64) for 1,800 GPU-hours, while continuing with direct reinforcement learning instead reaches only 67.6 pass@1 (90.0 pass@64) for 17,920 GPU-hours — roughly 10× the compute for a worse result. RL leaves pass@64 completely unmoved from the pre-RL checkpoint (90.0 → 90.0), while on-policy distillation pushes it further (90.0 → 93.3), which the report reads as evidence that a strong teacher expands the student's exploration space in a way RL alone does not.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

Two of this report's claims are genuinely checkable and hold up well: the thinking-budget scaling curves (Figure 2) are a real inference-time mechanism, not a marketing artifact, since the model was never explicitly trained to obey a budget; and the on-policy-distillation-vs-RL comparison (Table 21) is a controlled, same-checkpoint, same-benchmark ablation with a genuinely large effect size (10× compute for a worse result). Everything downstream of those two results, though, leans on evaluation infrastructure the report never opens up.

Start with contamination. Qwen2.5's own technical report disclosed an explicit longest-common-subsequence decontamination rule applied to pre- and post-training data. Qwen3's report — across all 35 pages — never mentions decontamination, contamination, or leakage once outside of two bibliography entries for LiveCodeBench and LiveBench (both cited as evidence other benchmarks are contamination-resistant, not as evidence Qwen3's own training data was checked against them). With a 36-trillion-token corpus assembled partly by OCR-ing "extensive PDF documents" and partly by synthesizing data with Qwen's own models, the omission is notable given the precedent the team itself set one release earlier.

Second, a meaningful slice of the internal evidence for why the four post-training stages help rests on four in-house benchmarks — CounterFactQA, LengthCtrl, ThinkFollow, ToolUse — that are used in Table 22 to justify Stage 3 and Stage 4, but never released or described beyond one sentence each. Table 22 is also refreshingly honest about a real cost: for Qwen3-32B's thinking mode, Stage 3 and Stage 4 together lower AIME'24 from 83.8 (after Stage 2) to 81.4 and LiveCodeBench v5 from 68.4 to 65.7, a trade-off the report accepts explicitly rather than hiding — a genuine point in its favor, and a rarer disclosure than most flagship releases make.

Third, competitor numbers throughout are self-run: "All models are evaluated using the same evaluation pipeline," which controls for prompt/harness variance but means every comparison number for DeepSeek-V3, Llama-4, and Gemma-3 is Qwen's own reproduction, not each lab's official reported score — and the comparison set itself shifts from table to table (Grok-3-Beta and Gemini 2.5 Pro appear only in the thinking-mode table; GPT-4o and Llama-4 only in the non-thinking one), so no single table lets a reader see the full competitive field at once. No error bars or repeated-run variance are reported anywhere except a bare mention that GPQA-Diamond is "sampled 10 times... and report[s] the averaged accuracy" — useful, but the only such disclosure in the paper, and even there no spread is given.

baselines

Current and mostly reasonable (DeepSeek-V3/R1, Llama-4, Gemma-3, o1/o3, Gemini 2.5 Pro), but self-run through Qwen's own harness rather than official reported numbers, and the comparison set changes between the thinking-mode and non-thinking-mode tables.

contamination

Not addressed anywhere in the report — a regression from Qwen2.5's disclosed LCS-based decontamination rule. With 36T tokens including OCR'd PDFs and self-synthesized data, overlap with the 15+ eval benchmarks used is simply unaudited.

what's missed

Four in-house benchmarks (CounterFactQA, LengthCtrl, ThinkFollow, ToolUse) back a chunk of the Stage-3/4 ablation story but are neither released nor specified beyond one line; no variance/error bars are reported anywhere except a single mention for GPQA-Diamond.

would raise confidence

Publish (or at least describe in detail) the in-house eval sets; disclose whatever decontamination check, if any, was run against the training corpus; and report repeated-run variance given how close some benchmark deltas are (e.g., the half-point-scale gaps between adjacent Qwen3 sizes).

// bottom line

The two headline mechanisms — emergent thinking-budget control and the distillation-beats-RL efficiency result — are real, controlled findings that should generalize beyond this report. But the surrounding evaluation apparatus is less rigorous than Qwen's own prior release: no decontamination disclosure, self-run baselines with a shifting comparison set, and internal ablations partly resting on benchmarks nobody outside Alibaba can inspect.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. One checkpoint, an inference-time reasoning dial. Thinking mode and non-thinking mode are fused into a single model via /think / /no_think flags, and the thinking-budget mechanism that lets a user cap reasoning length "emerges naturally" from Thinking Mode Fusion without being explicitly trained.
  2. On-policy distillation beats direct RL by roughly 10× compute. From the same starting checkpoint, distillation reaches 74.4 AIME'24 pass@1 for 1,800 GPU-hours versus RL's 67.6 for 17,920 GPU-hours — and only distillation moves pass@64 (90.0 → 93.3; RL: 90.0 → 90.0).
  3. A transparency step backward from Qwen2.5. The prior report disclosed an explicit LCS-based decontamination rule; Qwen3's 35-page report never mentions decontamination at all, and several internal ablations lean on four undisclosed in-house benchmarks.
